title: Know your REAL carry distances
microFact: Amateurs remember their best-ever 7-iron. Courses only care about your average one. Measure it.
lesson:
Ask an amateur their 7-iron distance and they quote their career strike. Then they stand at 150m, pull the 150m-best-case club, and come up short into the front bunker — the single most common amateur miss.

The fix is one range session with any measuring tool (a launch-monitor bay in Bangalore, a rangefinder, or GPS apps like Hole19/18Birdies on the course):

1. Hit 10 balls with each club, ignore the 2 best and 2 worst.
2. Record the AVERAGE CARRY (flight distance, not roll) of the middle six.
3. Write the numbers on a card in your bag or in your phone.

Expect sobering data — a typical 28-handicap carries a 7-iron ~120m, not the 145m of memory. That honesty is a superpower: suddenly approach shots finish pin-high, and pin-high changes everything.

Carry vs TOTAL matters: carry clears the bunker; total is where you finish. Firm Indian summer fairways add serious roll; monsoon-soft ones add none.

This card also makes byte 45's layup system real: you can't 'leave yourself 80' without knowing which club carries 130. Data first, strategy second.

quiz.question: Which number should drive your club choice for an approach over a bunker?
options: Your best-ever distance with the club
Your average TOTAL distance including roll
The number your partner hits that club
quiz.explanation: Carry is what clears trouble. Averages, not highlights — build the card and trust it over memory.
